serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sweet and sour prawn is approximately 200 g, comprising stir-fried prawn pieces with pineapple chunks and green pepper in sweet and sour sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and Sour Prawn — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sweet and Sour Prawn (CR ID: F03060036). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet and sour prawn as a composite dish. GI estimated at 51 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database convention) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: sweet and sour sauce (sugar-based, GI ~65) and pineapple (GI ~59), moderated by the protein from prawns and dietary fibre at 2.87g/100g which attenuates glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as (51 × 13.31 × 2) / 100 = 13.6g. This is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for sugar-sweetened sauces and tropical fruit.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium at 80 mg/100g with meaningful dietary fibre at 2.87 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 80 mg/100g is well within the low-sodium threshold; however, patients should be cautioned that commercial sweet and sour sauce preparations may contain substantially higher sodium, and portion control is advised to limit cumulative intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.52 g/100g from sweet and sour sauce with moderate carbohydrate load of 13.31 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high proportion of added sugar (7.52 g/100g) relative to total carbohydrate (13.31 g/100g) elevates the glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, request sauce on the side, and pair with high-fibre vegetables or brown rice to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat at 8.12 g/100g with low sodium at 80 mg/100g and prawn-derived lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Total fat of 8.12 g/100g is moderate and prawn protein is lean; the primary cardiovascular concern is the added sugar from the sauce, which at 7.52 g/100g may contribute to triglyceride elevation with frequent consumption, so portion moderation is recommended for CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Green bell peppers are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should consider requesting this dish without green bell peppers or substituting with non-nightshade vegetables such as broccoli or snap peas.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Prawn is a popular Singaporean Chinese stir-fried seafood dish consisting of prawn meat cooked with pineapple chunks and green bell peppers in a glossy sweet and sour sauce typically made from tomato ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, and sometimes plum sauce. The dish delivers a characteristic balance of tangy acidity and sweetness with a tender-crisp texture from the prawns and a slight crunch from the peppers. Per 100g, it provides 156 kcal, 7.44g protein, 8.12g fat, 13.31g carbohydrate, 7.52g sugar, 2.87g dietary fibre, 80mg sodium, 61mg calcium, and 1.98mg iron. The relatively low sodium content (80mg/100g) makes it suitable for hypertension management when prepared at home, though commercial preparations may contain higher sodium. The added sugar from the sauce (7.52g/100g) is a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management, and the dish carries a low glycaemic index estimated at 51. The green bell peppers are nightshade vegetables, which may be a consideration for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is commonly served as part of a shared Chinese meal in Singapore hawker centres, zi char restaurants, and home kitchens, and is suitable for halal diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a confidence of 0.95 for an exact match to the named dish (F03060036). All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present from the primary approved source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 51 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for sugar-sweetened sauces and tropical fruit components; no direct GI measurement for sweet and sour prawn was found in the approved GI database. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification convention, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'; therefore gi_category is set to 'low' for glycemic_index 51. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.375 from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; this has been replaced with the analogous estimate. Occasion tag 'snack' from the input has been reclassified to meal context entries 'lunch' and 'dinner' as this is a main-course dish. Dietary tag 'low_sodium' from the input is supported by the 80mg/100g sodium value and has been preserved in dietary_flags context.
